title: Pigmentation and dark spots
excerpt: Dark spots and uneven skin tone are ancient concerns in Ayurveda, understood as Vyanga, a pitta-vata imbalance that dims the face's natural radiance. Classical texts offer cooling herbs, sensible sun habits, and time-tested face pastes that work gently over weeks.
at_a_glance: Vyanga is the Ayurvedic name for facial pigmentation, linked to excess pitta and vata imbalance in the skin.
Cooling foods, consistent sleep, and time-tested face pastes address both the root cause and the surface discolouration.
Sun protection is classical advice, not modern marketing: direct midday sun aggravates pitta and deepens spots.

body.heading: that stubborn patch on your cheek
paragraphs: Most of us notice it slowly: a shadow near the upper lip, a flat brown spot on the cheekbone, or a cluster of dark patches that appeared after a bout of acne or a summer of forgetting sunscreen. We cover it with foundation and move on, but the question stays: why does the skin hold on to colour this way, and is there anything gentler than a chemical peel?

Ayurveda named this condition Vyanga thousands of years ago. The word describes a discolouration of the face, particularly on the cheeks and forehead, that appears gradually and without pain. Sushruta classified Vyanga among the minor skin conditions (kshudra rogas) arising from an imbalance of pitta and vata doshas (Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana). Pitta governs heat and colour metabolism in the skin; vata governs circulation and the skin's suppleness. When pitta is aggravated, by summer heat, spicy food, or stress, and vata dries out the surface, the skin's natural pigment cells go into overdrive, depositing melanin unevenly.

body.heading: what classical texts say is happening
paragraphs: Ashtanga Hridaya, one of the core Ayurvedic references, places skin colour and lustre under the category of prabha, the inner radiance that reflects the health of rakta dhatu (blood tissue) and rasa dhatu (plasma). When these tissues carry excess pitta, the skin shows it as hyperpigmentation, dullness, or reddish-brown patches (Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana).

Bhavaprakasha, the sixteenth-century encyclopaedia of Ayurvedic medicine, describes the skin's colour as deeply connected to the quality of digestion. A sluggish or irregular agni (digestive fire) produces ama, a metabolic residue that circulates in rasa dhatu and settles in the skin, contributing to uneven tone and pigmentation (Bhavaprakasha, Purva Khanda). The text recommends both internal correction through diet and external application of cooling, complexion-clearing herbs.

Sushruta's surgical tradition also offers a practical view: the skin has seven layers, each with distinct functions. Pigmentation disturbances, when superficial, respond well to external therapies called lepa (medicated face packs). Deeper discolouration may need internal medicine and dietary change to address the root cause in rakta dhatu (Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana).

body.heading: everyday habits that actually help
paragraphs: Cooling pitta is the first goal. This means reducing foods that generate heat: very spicy curries, excessive fermented foods, alcohol, and midday sun exposure without protection. Favour cooling foods like cucumber, coconut water, coriander leaves, fennel seeds, and seasonal fruits. A simple pitta-pacifying habit is to drink a glass of room-temperature water with a pinch of dried coriander powder first thing in the morning.

For the skin itself, classical texts consistently recommend Chandan (sandalwood), Lodhra bark, Manjishtha root, and Kumari (aloe vera) as safe, time-tested options for brightening and cooling. A traditional lepa can be made by mixing chickpea flour with a little raw milk and a pinch of turmeric to form a paste, applied to clean skin for 10 to 15 minutes before washing off. Bhavaprakasha specifically notes turmeric's effect on improving skin complexion through its action on rakta (Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu). Consistency over weeks matters more than intensity.

Sun sense is non-negotiable. Classical texts were written for a hot climate and repeatedly caution against excessive exposure of the face to direct midday sun (Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana). A cotton dupatta, a wide-brimmed hat, or simply shifting outdoor activity to early morning and late evening protects the skin from further pitta aggravation without any product at all.

Sleep and stress management are underrated. Vata rises with irregular sleep and mental agitation, which disturbs circulation to the skin. Going to bed before 10:30 pm, eating dinner at a consistent time, and a five-minute oil self-massage (abhyanga) on the face with a few drops of coconut or almond oil before bed all help stabilise vata and keep the skin's circulation even.

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: This article describes general Ayurvedic principles and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. If your dark patches are spreading rapidly, appeared suddenly without an obvious cause, are accompanied by itching, bleeding, or changes in texture, or if they are affecting your emotional wellbeing significantly, please see a qualified dermatologist or a registered Ayurvedic vaidya. Melasma, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, and other conditions have specific causes and treatments that a professional can identify accurately. Conditions like Addison's disease, certain medication reactions, and rare skin disorders can also cause pigmentation changes and require prompt medical evaluation. Do not delay medical care based on home remedies alone.

try_this_today.title: Morning coriander water
try_this_today.description: Add a small pinch of dried coriander powder to a glass of room-temperature water. Drink it first thing in the morning on an empty stomach. Coriander is considered cooling to pitta and is a gentle daily ritual for skin clarity.

try_this_today.title: Chickpea and turmeric lepa
try_this_today.description: Mix two tablespoons of chickpea flour (besan) with enough raw milk to form a paste, then add a small pinch of turmeric. Apply to clean, dry skin on the face. Leave for 10 to 15 minutes, then rinse with cool water. Use two to three times a week over several weeks.

try_this_today.title: Evening face oil
try_this_today.description: Before bed, warm a few drops of cold-pressed coconut or sweet almond oil between your palms and massage gently into the face for two to three minutes using upward circular strokes. This supports vata balance and improves overnight circulation to the skin.

try_this_today.title: Shift outdoor time
try_this_today.description: Move walks, exercise, and outdoor errands to before 9 am or after 5 pm. The midday sun between 11 am and 3 pm is the peak pitta aggravation window for skin. A cotton dupatta or hat is the simplest and most classical sun-protection tool.

try_this_today.title: Cool your dinner plate
try_this_today.description: For one month, reduce very spicy, fried, and fermented foods at dinner. Replace with lighter options: dal with a tempering of cumin and coriander, steamed vegetables, and a small cup of buttermilk (takra) with a pinch of cumin. This directly reduces pitta load in rakta dhatu.

ingredients.name: Coriander
ingredients.description: Cooling to pitta; used internally as a morning drink and externally in traditional skin preparations to reduce heat in the skin.

ingredients.name: Fennel
ingredients.description: Gentle pitta-pacifying seed; chewed after meals to support digestion and reduce the internal heat that contributes to pigmentation.

ingredients.name: Cumin
ingredients.description: Supports agni without aggravating pitta when used in moderate amounts; a common tempering spice in the cooling diet recommended for Vyanga.

words_to_know.definition: Facial pigmentation or dark patches; one of the minor skin conditions (kshudra rogas) described in Sushruta Samhita, caused by pitta-vata imbalance.
The dosha governing heat, metabolism, and colour in the body; when excess, it can cause skin inflammation and uneven pigmentation.
Blood tissue; the bodily tissue most closely linked to skin colour and complexion in Ayurvedic physiology.
A medicated paste or face pack applied externally; a major treatment category for skin conditions in classical Ayurvedic texts.
Undigested metabolic residue formed when agni (digestive fire) is weak; believed to circulate through tissues and contribute to dullness and discolouration.
The natural inner radiance or lustre of the skin, reflecting the health of rasa and rakta dhatu.

sources: Sushruta Samhita, Chikitsa Sthana (classification and management of Vyanga among kshudra rogas) · Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana (seven-layer model of the skin and lepa therapy) · Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana (sun exposure caution for facial skin) · Bhavaprakasha, (role of agni and ama in skin discolouration and the value of internal and external therapies for complexion) · Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu (haridra's action on rakta and skin complexion)
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
